course_title: The Roman Empire: Politics and Society

learning_outcomes.description: Understand major events and personalities in the Roman imperial period, from the succession of Tiberius (14 CE) to the Severan Dynasty (193-235 CE).
Understand the major political, social, administrative, and religious institutions of the Roman Empire and how these evolved in the period studied.
Critically analyse key ancient texts for Roman imperial history, including Tacitus' Annals and Cassius Dio's Roman History.
Critically analyse non-literary forms of evidence, including inscriptions and coins, and assess their value for the Roman historian.
Appreciate how interactions between Romans and non-Romans helped to shape the Roman Empire.
Appreciate the ways in which imperial Rome has influenced the modern world.
Communicate effectively with teachers and peers in discussion groups.
Conduct independent research, manage information, and communicate findings in a coherent and scholarly manner.
Use computers and information technology effectively.

assessments.due_date: By 2pm Friday in the week following the relevant tutorial
assessments.task_description:
Students are required to research and write a scholarly essay of 2,000 words (+/- 10%, excluding bibliography and references) on one of the essay questions included in the Tutorial and Essay Programme (on Blackboard). The essay questions are linked to the weekly tutorial topics. You will be asked to sign up for an essay topic at the start of semester. You only need to write one essay. You must submit an essay in order to pass the course.

Essay sign-up is on a first-in basis via Blackboard. Sign-up opens in Week 1. Please choose your topic by the end of Week 3. If you have not signed up for a topic by the end of Week 3, you will be randomly allocated to one of the remaining available topics. If you need to change your topic after Week 3, you will need to write to the course coordinator to request a change (subject to availability).

The essay must be submitted by 2pm Friday in the week after the tutorial week at which that topic was discussed. For example, if you choose to write on the topic for Week 2, you must submit your essay by 2pm (14:00) on Friday in Week 3. The exact due date for each essay topic can be found in the Tutorial and Essay Programme. Please take account of the due date when choosing an essay topic and plan your time accordingly.

A reading list for each topic can also be found in the Tutorial and Essay Programme. It is essential that you use the required reading in writing your essay (ancient and modern).

Your essay must be fully referenced following the conventions set out in the Classics and Ancient History Study Guide and Study Videos (on Blackboard), including specific chapter/section numbers for ancient sources and specific page numbers for modern works. The Study Guide and Videos also include detailed guidance on planning and writing Ancient History essays.

It is highly recommended that you do not use AI in researching or writing your essay. Any AI use must be referenced.

 

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit through the relevant Turnitin link on Blackboard
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
There are ten tutorials in ANCH2230, starting in Week 2. A detailed Tutorial Programme, including reading lists, will be made available via Blackboard. You should sign up for a tutorial group before Week 1 and remain in that group for the entire semester. Tutorials are conducted in person and are not recorded.

Tutorials are an integral part of the course, since they offer the opportunity for small-group discussion of specific topics, close analysis of ancient evidence, and completion of individual and group activities. Exam questions will also address material covered in tutorials.

You are expected to prepare for each tutorial by completing the required reading and preparing notes based on the discussion questions for each week. The questions are designed to guide your reading and preparation for the tutorial. It is recommended that you bring notes on paper (handwritten or printed) if you wish to refer to them during class, as some activities will be completed without use of electronic devices (laptops, tablets, phones, etc.). Please note that use of AI tools during tutorials is prohibited and relying on AI tools is likely to hinder your learning and skill-development.

Tutorial activities will vary by week and may include worksheets, creative exercises, reflections, source analysis, and mini quizzes. Some activities are designed to be completed in small groups and some individually. Some will be completed 'closed book' and/or without use of electronic devices (secure assessment). Details of each activity will be provided in class. These activities will not be graded separately but will be taken into account in evaluating the quality and extent of your preparation for and engagement in tutorials.

Tutorial participation and activities are worth 20% of your overall mark for the course. You are expected to participate in (and therefore attend) all ten tutorials. While missing one or two tutorials over the semester will not have a serious impact on your mark, poor attendance will limit your learning as well as your participation mark, and failure to participate in at least one tutorial will result in an automatic grade of 2. That is, you must participate in at least one of the ten tutorials in order to pass the course.

Because tutorial exercises are secure assessment, they must be completed in person. If you are unable to attend due to illness or misadventure and you wish to make up participation for that week, please contact the course coordinator as soon as possible to discuss alternative arrangements and obtain a medical certificate (if relevant). Alternative arrangements may include attending a make-up session or an oral interview. It is not acceptable to send notes instead of participating in person unless by prior arrangement with the course coordinator.

If you have an SAP that may impact how you engage in this assessment, please contact the course coordinator as soon as possible to discuss appropriate accommodations.

 

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
The exam will be held on campus during the exam period. The exam will consist of short-answer questions, a source analysis exercise (in the form of a short essay), and an essay. Exam questions will be based on lecture and tutorial content.

Further details will be provided in class and on Blackboard.

You must sit the exam in order to pass the course.

 

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
